title: Gut Reset Procedure May Prevent Weight Regain After Stopping GLP-1 Drugs
summary: A new study presented at Digestive Disease Week 2026 suggests that a minimally invasive procedure called duodenal mucosal resurfacing (DMR) could help people maintain weight loss after stopping GLP-1 medications like Ozempic or Wegovy. About 70% of people who stop these drugs regain much of the weight within 18 months. The REMAIN-1 trial tested DMR in 45 participants who had lost at least 15% of their body weight using tirzepatide. Those who received the actual procedure regained significantly less weight after six months compared to the sham group. The procedure uses controlled heat to remove damaged tissue from the duodenum, promoting growth of healthier tissue. No serious complications were reported, and recovery takes about one day. The benefits appeared to increase over time, suggesting a lasting metabolic reset. This could address the huge unmet need for a treatment that allows patients to stop GLP-1 drugs without regaining weight.

background_read: GLP-1 medications like Ozempic and Wegovy are effective for weight loss, but many patients stop due to cost, side effects, or desire to avoid long-term medication. When they stop, about 70% regain significant weight within 18 months. Duodenal mucosal resurfacing is an investigational endoscopic procedure that ablates the damaged mucosal layer of the duodenum, promoting regeneration of healthier tissue. The REMAIN-1 trial is a blinded, randomized, sham-controlled study evaluating whether DMR can maintain weight loss after discontinuation of GLP-1 therapy. Early results show that DMR reduces weight regain, with benefits increasing over time. No serious complications occurred, and the procedure is minimally invasive with quick recovery.
Article_Structure: The article reports on the REMAIN-1 trial investigating duodenal mucosal resurfacing (DMR) for weight maintenance after GLP-1 cessation. Main points: DMR reduced weight regain compared to sham; benefits increased over time; procedure is safe and minimally invasive. Purpose: To address the unmet need for a treatment that prevents weight regain after stopping GLP-1 drugs. Evidence: Blinded, randomized, sham-controlled trial with 45 participants; objective weight measurements; statistical significance. Author credibility: Dr. Shelby Sullivan is director of an endoscopic bariatric program and professor at Dartmouth, indicating relevant expertise. Methodology: Endoscopic ablation of duodenal mucosa; follow-up at six months; sham control; dose-response analysis.

perspectives.description: Researchers are excited about the potential of DMR to provide a lasting metabolic reset, with dose-response effects suggesting biological targeting.
Patients who stop GLP-1 drugs face high risk of weight regain; DMR offers a possible solution to maintain benefits without ongoing medication.
